nameMeanings.name: Hody
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Brendan, Caitlin, Conor, Declan, Thomas Patrick.
Irish (Louth): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEochaidh ‘descendant of Eochaidh’, a variant of Eachaidh, a personal name meaning ‘horseman’ (a derivative of each ‘horse’).
Norwegian: habitational name from a farmstead named Hoøy from Old Norse hór ‘high’ + ey ‘island’. This surname is very rare in Norway.

variants.name: Hoy
meanings: English (Essex): nickname for a sailor, from Middle Dutch hoey ‘cargo ship’.
Danish (Høy): nickname for a tall person, from høj ‘high’.

variants.name: Haughey
meanings: Irish (Tyrone): variant of Hoey from Ó hEachaidh. Compare McCaughey .

variants.name: Haugh
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Irish Connor, Eamon.
Irish (Clare): Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEachach ‘descendant of Eochu’, a personal name which is probably a pet form of Eochaidh, Eachaidh (see Hoey ).

Scottish and English: from northern Middle English and Older Scots halh, haugh ‘a piece of flat alluvial land by the side of a river, typically one deposited in a bend’, also ‘river-meadow land’; either topographic for someone who lived near such a feature or habitational for someone who came from one of several places in Scotland and northern England called Haugh.

variants.name: Haughn
meanings: Irish: shortened Anglicized form of Gaelic Ó hEacháin ‘descendant of Eachán’, probably a diminutive of Eachaidh (see Hoey ).

variants.name: Xu
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Chinese: Wei, Jing, Jian, Ming, Li, Yi, Hui, Bin, Gang, Hong, Ping, Jin, Min, Chong, You, Chang, Hu, Neng, Shen, Yiming, Yiping.  Vietnamese Long, Hao, Lan, Hai, Bian, Buu, Cong, Dai, Mai, Nu, Quan, Thi.

Mandarin form of the surname 徐, meaning ‘gradually, slowly’ in Chinese: (i) from Xu (徐), the name of a state (covering present-day Jiangsu and Anhui province) originally granted to Ruo Mu, son of an official called Bo Yi during the reign of Xia Yu, the first king of the Xia dynasty (2070–1600  BC ). The state was destroyed by the state of Chu during the Western Zhou dynasty (1046–771  BC ) but later restored and eventually annexed by the state of Wu in 512  BC . After its annihilation, the name of the state was adopted as a surname. (ii) borne by the descendants of the Shang dynasty (1600–1046  BC ). The Xu family was one of the six clans of the Shang dynasty that were sent to the state of Lu after the annihilation of the Shang dynasty. (iii) surname borne by descendants of Xu Zhi Gao (徐知誥), an alternative name of Li Sheng, the founder of the state of Southern Tang (937–975  AD ). (iv) a surname of the Xu (徐) family from the Di ethnic group in northern China.

nameMeanings.name: Richard
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: One of the most enduringly successful of the Old French personal names introduced into Britain by the Normans. It is of Germanic (Frankish) origin, derived from rīc ‘power’ + hard ‘strong, hardy’. It has enjoyed continuous popularity in England from the Conquest to the present day, influenced by the fact that it was borne by three kings of England, in particular Richard I ( 1157–99 ). He was king for only ten years ( 1189–99 ), most of which he spent in warfare abroad, taking part in the Third Crusade and costing the people of England considerable sums in taxes. Nevertheless, he achieved the status of a folk hero, and was never in England long enough to disappoint popular faith in his goodness and justice. He was also Duke of Aquitaine and Normandy and Count of Anjou, fiefs which he held at a time of maximum English expansion in France. His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

variants.name: Rikert

His exploits as a leader of the Third Crusade earned him the nickname ‘Coeur de Lion’ or ‘Lionheart’ and a permanent place in popular imagination, in which he was even more firmly enshrined by Sir Walter Scott's novel Ivanhoe ( 1820 ).

historicEvents.title: Famine Strikes Europe
historicEvents.description: A famine caused by cold winters and wet summers across Europe caused millions to die due to starvation. Desperation from hunger caused high levels of crime, stampedes in bread lines in London, infanticide, and even cannibalism throughout Europe.

historicEvents.title: First War of Scottish Independence
historicEvents.description: The First War of Scottish Independence began after England invaded Scotland in 1296. The Treaty of Edinburgh was signed in 1328 which declared peace between the two kingdoms and brought the war to an end.

historicEvents.title: The Hundred Years' War
historicEvents.description: The Hundred Years' War between France and England was largely due to the question of legitimate succession to the French crown.

historicEvents.title: Wycliffe's Bible
historicEvents.description: John Wycliffe, English theologian and professor at the University of Oxford, completed a translation of the Bible from the Latin Vulgate into Middle English. This translation is known as Wycliffe's Bible and was completed around 1382.

en.lifeSummary: When Richard Hody was born about 1310, in Emlyn, Carmarthenshire, Wales, his father, Richard Hody I, was 27 and his mother, Mrs Richard Hody I, was 22. He had at least 1 son and 2 daughters with Margaret Wormegay. He died in 1399, in Gloucestershire, England, at the age of 90.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque Richard Hody est né environ 1310, à Emlyn, Carmarthenshire, Galles, son père, Richard Hody I, avait 27 ans et sa mère, Mrs Richard Hody I, avait 22 ans. Il a eu au moins 1 fils et 2 filles avec Margaret Wormegay. Il est décédé en 1399, à Gloucestershire, Angleterre, à l’âge de 90 ans.

Lui avuto almeno 1 figlio e 2 figlie con Margaret Wormegay. Morì nel 1399, nel Gloucestershire, England, all'età di 90 anni.
